title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
Pre-season forecasts

The pre-season forecast for the Total Return of Skeena sockeye in 2026 ranges from 728,000 to 3.3 million (90% confidence range) with a point estimate (p50) of 1.5 million.
On average, Alaskan fisheries take approximately 5-10% of the aggregate sockeye run, but this depends on their pink salmon fisheries and effort, and can be much higher in some years.

Tyee Test Fishery

Information from fisheries and the Tyee test fishery suggest that sockeye are mostly large this year, suggesting a high proportion of 5-year olds.
To date 1,022,556 sockeye are estimated to have passed the Tyee test fishery.
We are nearing the historical mid-point (peak) of the run through Tyee. While early estimates of final run size were encouraging, the run-timing model forecast for TRTC continues to decline with a final TRTC estimate of 2.5M based on average run-timing (1.7M if the run is one week early, and 4.3M if the run is one week late).
The shape of the run-timing curve suggests that the run could be near the forecast at 1.5M, and that there was a strong and early start to the run.
The daily expected run through Tyee at a final run size of 2M and average run timing would be about 55-60,000.
While run-timing is one source of uncertainty in this estimate, catchability is also a large source of uncertainty. Uncertainty due to run-timing will decrease through the season, where the final catchability of the test fishery is only known post-season. The mid-point of the sockeye run is approximately July 24-26, when the certainty of the model forecast will increase.
The Chinook cumulative index is well below the historical median, and just above the recent 5-year mean and 10-year mean, suggesting continued low abundance for Skeena Chinook.

Babine

The Babine fence started operations on July 15.
Sockeye have started coming through the fence in very low numbers.

Kitwanga Fence

The Kitwanga fence starts operations in mid-July. Information from the Kitwanga fence will be shared weekly by Gitanyow Fisheries Authority and links to their reports for 2026 will be updated below.

First Nations Food Fisheries

First Nations food fisheries are ongoing in the marine approaches and in-river.

Commercial Fisheries

First Nations demonstration fisheries opened in the marine areas on July 8 and 9 targeting sockeye and are ongoing.
First Nations demonstration fisheries opened in-river July 16 and are ongoing.
Area C gillnet fisheries started July 10 targeting Skeena sockeye. There were openings on July 10/11, 13/14, 16/17, 19/20/21 and openings continue July 23/24/25.
Area A seine fisheries targeting Skeena sockeye opened July 15-19 and continue July 22-26.
Area 3 seine fisheries opened July 7 and 8 targeting Nass pink salmon, with pink salmon catches being extremely low. Sockeye and chum retention is allowed, with decent catches of chum.

Recreational Fisheries

Recreational fisheries for sockeye in Areas 3-6 opened July 10 with a daily limit of 4 per day.
Recreational fisheries for Skeena sockeye in portions of Region 6 opened July 10 (portions of the Skeena mainstem) and August 1 (Babine Lake, Fulton River, Pinkut Creek) with a daily limit of 2 per day.
Recreational fisheries for Chinook in Area 3-5 are currently closed, and will open at 1 per day on July 18, and 2 per day on August 1.

Appendix A shows the daily and cumulative run-timing of Tyee since 2000 versus 2024.
Appendix B will include information on the other species caught in the Area 4 commercial fishery and CPUEs and will be updated once fisheries begin.
Appendix C includes information on run-timing of specific Skeena Conservation Units/stocks.

Sockeye
The pre-season forecast for the Total Return of Skeena sockeye in 2026 ranges from 728,000 to 3.3 million (90% confidence range) with a point estimate (p50) of 1.5 million.



2026 Sockeye Total Return Forecast. Source: NC STAD Charmaine Carr-Harris Post-Season Review Presentation.

title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
Chinook
The Chinook forecast for 2026 is similar to last year, at ~ 33,000
The table below shows the total 2026 Skeena Chinook terminal run point forecast (highlighted in pink) and forecasted returns by age. Age-specific forecasts are obtained using ForecastR, which uses a model selection and ranking approach and retrospective forecast performance.



2026 Skeena Chinook forecast

title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
Sockeye Salmon

Sockeye index
The 2026 sockeye index is shown below and compared to historical index data from the Tyee test fishery (Figure 1).








Figure 1: Daily (top) and cumulative (bottom) index values for sockeye salmon from 1956-2025 (grey lines). 2026 values are shown in purple, and various percentiles are shown by the grey dashed lines.







Escapement estimates at Tyee
Escapement estimates for sockeye are generated using the Tyee index and a catchability coefficient based on the average of the last 3 years catchability.
The 2025 sockeye escapement estimate at Tyee is shown below and compared to historical escapement data (not including catch as we do not have the historical daily escapement estimate plus catch) (Figure 2)








Figure 2: Daily (top) and cumulative (bottom) escapement estimates at Tyee for sockeye salmon from 1970-2025 (grey lines). 2026 values are shown in purple, and various percentiles are shown by the grey dashed lines.







Cumulative index by year for today’s date





Cumulative index for sockeye for today’s date by year for 1956-2025, with rolling 10-year average shown by the black line and the recent 5-year average shown by the red line.






Daily and cumulative escapement for recent years (2021-2025) vs. 2026








Figure 3: Daily (top panel) and cumulative (bottom panel) sockeye escapement for 2021-2026.







In-season abundance estimate
Sockeye in-season abundance (TRTC) is estimated through the Tyee test fishery using the cumulative run-to Tyee (escapement past Tyee plus adjusted marine catch estimates) and run-timing. The following table (Table 1) and figure (Figure 4) show estimates of the final forecasted TRTC for average, one-week late, and one-week early run-timing. This does not incorporate variability in the catchability of the Tyee test fishery (which is confirmed post-season).


[1] "Model estimates as of July 23"




Table 1: The % of run past Tyee and the associated TRTC estimate for average, one-week early, and one-week late run-timing.






Run-timing
Run to Tyee to Date
% of Run Through

Figure 4: Final estimated TRTC from the simple sockeye in-season abundance estimator for early (green line), average (red line) and late (blue line) run-timing. The dashed line shows the pre-season forecast of a TRTC of ~1.5 million.













Figure 5: Comparison of sockeye cumulative proportions from 1956-2025 to this year using different run past Tyee estimates.

title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
North Coast Salmon Updates
DFO Stock Assessment and Resource Management provide stakeholders a weekly update every Tuesday. These updates contain information for Skeena salmon, as well as other North Coast and Central Coast areas, including fisheries catch numbers, in-season forecasts, and other relevant information.
Update #1-June 30
Update #2-July 7
Update #3-July 14
Update #4-July 21

title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
Commercial Fisheries

Area C Gillnet and Area A Seine
The following section shows the harvest control rule for commercial sockeye fisheries in the Skeena, the estimated TAC for various run-timings, and the available information on catches in the Area 4 commercial marine gillnet and seine fisheries targeting sockeye at the mouth of the Skeena River (generally sub-areas 4-12 and 4-15). Information should be considered preliminary until reviewed by DFO staff in the post-season.








Figure 20: The Skeena sockeye harvest control rule based on TRTC. The vertical lines show the estimated TRTC for 2026 by run-timing (red=early, green=average, blue=late).













Figure 21: Cumulative Area 4 gillnet and seine sockeye catch versus daily estimates of TAC based on early (blue), average (red), and late (green) run-timing from the in-season TRTC model.













Figure 22: Top panel: Area 4 gillnet and seine sockeye catch. Bottom panel: CPUE of gillnet and seine based on daily catch/daily effort. Note that some values are preliminary.













Figure 23: Area 4 gillnet and seine catch of all species by day and disposition (kept and released). Data is preliminary and provided by DFO Resource Management.













Figure 24: Area 4 gillnet and seine CPUE (catch per unit effort) of all species by day and gear type. Data is preliminary and provided by DFO Resource Management.





The following tables show the catch and CPUE of salmon and steelhead caught in the Area 4 gillnet and seine commercial fisheries (not including First Nation’s demonstration fisheries), as well as the total kept and released for the year to date for each species.

First Nations Demonstration and Economic Opportunity
First Nations CSAF demonstration fisheries opened on July 8 in 2026. The catch associated with each demonstration fishery is determined using a proportion of the weekly TAC. In 2026, the CTAC approach is being implemented, which means that the catch allocation of the demonstration fishery is no longer directly linked to what the commercial marine fishery catches each week. Catch by Nation or Nation group and gear type are shown below.



Preliminary Area 4 First Nations demo catches of sockeye.

title: Skeena Salmon In-Season Update
text:
Recreational Fisheries
The following table shows the updated management guidelines for recreational fisheries targeting sockeye in the Skeena watershed as per the 2026-27 North Coast Salmon IFMP. For the 2026 season, the recreational fishery for Skeena Sockeye will open when the estimated Total Return to Canada threshold of 1.05 million Sockeye has been achieved and commercial fisheries are triggered.



Guidelines for Management Actions for Recreational Sockeye Fisheries in the Skeena Watershed



North Coast Recreational Angling Creel Surveys
The North Coast Skeena First Nations Stewardship Society (NCSFNSS) has operated a creel survey program since 2015 in Areas 3 and 4, consisting of access point angler interviews and aerial overflights for the months of June through August (May through August beginning in 2019). Lodge-based recreational effort and harvest are monitored using DFO’s voluntary logbook program.
The following plots show the estimated recreational effort and catch for Chinook and coho salmon and Pacific halibut by year and month, and estimated CPUE for each species by year.








Figure 25: Estimates of recreational angling effort and catch (retained and released) for Chinook salmon, coho salmon, and Pacific halibut, by month and year (2016-2026). Data from 2026 is preliminary and is shown by the black outline.













Figure 26: Overall annual estimated effort and catch (retained and released) for Chinook salmon, coho salmon, and Pacific halibut (2016-2026).













Figure 27: Annual estimated CPUE for Chinook salmon, coho salmon, and Pacific halibut (2016-2026).
